title: Blood Feeding Protocol for Aedes aegypti
author: Steven Stenhouse

steps_raw.instruction: Keep defibrinated sheep blood refrigerated until ready for use.
Use a temperature-controlled heating unit to maintain the watch glass at a constant temperature during feeding. The device should provide stable, uniform heat approximating host body temperature.

Place one blood-feeding device in each cage for up to 24 hours. Inspect each device periodically and monitor blood temperature using an infrared thermometer to ensure consistent feeding conditions. Maintain blood temperature at 38 °C throughout the feeding period. Very little blood will be left in the watch glass of a well-fed cage.

To set up the water recirculator, begin by attaching the jacketed beaker to the inlet and outlet tubes of the heated water recirculator. Fill the recirculator with water, close the lid, plug in the machine, and set the temperature to 48 °C. Check the system to ensure there are no air bubbles in the recirculator, jacketed beaker, or tubing, as these can interfere with proper circulation. Once the pump begins operating and starts filling the beaker, open the port and add additional water as needed to maintain consistent flow and volume.

Pour the appropriate volume of blood into the jacketed beaker, depending on the number of watch glasses needed. Each watch glass requires approximately 8 mL of blood, so calculate the total volume accordingly to ensure sufficient supply.
Use a thermometer to monitor the blood temperature. The blood is ready for use when it reaches 45–48 °C.

To prepare the watch glasses, wrap each one with a piece of 25 cm² of parafilm. Use a cup as a support structure while wrapping to stabilize the glass. Stretch the parafilm taut across the top and secure it to the underside, creating a shallow pocket of air. Leave a small opening on one side to allow pipetting of blood into the pocket.

Place watch glasses with parafilm in the sink. Turn on tap water and peel the parafilm off the watch glass. Dispose of parafilm in a trash container. Remove any clotted blood or residue using gloves. Rinse the watch glass thoroughly and place it into a 1:100 dilution of 6% bleach solution. Allow glassware to soak for approximately 2 hours, then rinse with tap water, and air dry.

After feeding, carefully remove the device from each cage, blowing on them to try to dislodge as many females as possible. Separate heating sources from watch glasses. Put blood materials in a plastic tray and take to the wash area.
Once the blood has reached the target temperature, use a graduated pipette to measure 8 mL of blood.
Hold the watch glass vertically, with the parafilm opening facing upward, and pipette the blood into the pocket. This volume should fill approximately half of the pocket.
To remove excess air, gently tilt the watch glass forward and press on the parafilm with your thumb, taking care not to spill any blood.
Once most of the air has been expelled, seal the parafilm to the back of the watch glass.
Ensure the prepared watch glass sits flat on a surface (parafilm-side up) without leaking. Confirm that the blood is in contact with most of the parafilm surface to allow mosquitoes to feed easily.
Connect heat pads to the heating unit and secure them using appropriate hooks and plugs to ensure consistent contact and heat transfer.
Place prepared watch glasses containing blood directly onto the heating units to maintain the target temperature throughout the feeding period.
